prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 42-year-old Black woman.

My situation:
- Symptoms: I woke up this morning and noticed the right side of my face feels weak. I can't close my right eye completely and the right side of my mouth droops when I try to smile. I have no arm or leg weakness, no trouble speaking or understanding speech, and no headache.
- History: I had a mild cold about a week ago. No diabetes. No history of stroke or heart disease.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Sudden one-sided facial weakness with inability to fully close the eye and mouth droop can be caused by Bell’s palsy (often after a viral illness), but a stroke can sometimes look similar. Because this started suddenly today, it’s important to urgently rule out stroke and other serious causes, even though you don’t have arm/leg weakness or speech problems. If it is Bell’s palsy, treatment (often steroids, plus eye protection) works best when started early, and not being able to close the eye can risk eye injury/drying.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Sudden one-sided facial weakness with trouble closing the eye and mouth droop can be caused by Bell’s palsy (often after a recent viral illness), but it can also be a stroke. Even without arm/leg weakness or speech problems, stroke can sometimes present this way, and it’s important to rule it out quickly because treatment is time-sensitive. Bell’s palsy is usually treated early (often within 72 hours) with medicines and eye protection to prevent corneal damage from incomplete eyelid closure. Because this started this morning and you can’t fully close your eye, you should get urgent in-person evaluation now.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Sudden one-sided facial weakness with inability to fully close the eye can be caused by Bell’s palsy (often after a viral illness), but a stroke can sometimes look similar. Even though you have no arm/leg weakness or speech problems, the safest approach is to treat new facial droop as a possible stroke until a clinician rules it out. Time matters because stroke treatments are most effective early, and Bell’s palsy also benefits from starting treatment (often steroids) as soon as possible. Not being able to close the eye also risks eye injury and needs prompt protection and guidance.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Sudden one-sided facial weakness with inability to fully close the eye can be caused by Bell’s palsy (often after a recent viral illness), but a stroke can sometimes look similar. Because this started today and involves new facial droop, it’s safest to treat it as a possible stroke until a clinician rules that out. Time matters for stroke evaluation and treatment. Even if it is Bell’s palsy, early assessment is important because steroids are most helpful when started within about 72 hours, and you may need eye-protection measures to prevent corneal injury since you can’t close the eye fully.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Sudden one-sided facial weakness with inability to fully close the eye can be caused by Bell’s palsy (often after a viral illness), but it can also be a sign of a stroke. Even without arm/leg weakness or speech problems, stroke can sometimes present mainly with facial droop. Because this started today and is new, it needs urgent evaluation to rule out stroke and to start treatment promptly if it is Bell’s palsy (steroids are most helpful when started early). Also, not being able to close the eye fully can damage the cornea and may need immediate eye protection.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms sound like sudden one-sided facial weakness affecting the eye and mouth. This pattern can happen with Bell’s palsy (often after a viral illness), but a stroke can sometimes look similar and must be ruled out quickly. The fact that you can’t fully close the eye suggests the whole side of the face is involved, which fits Bell’s palsy, and you don’t report arm/leg weakness or speech problems, which is reassuring—but it doesn’t completely exclude stroke. Early medical evaluation matters because if this is Bell’s palsy, starting treatment (often steroids) within about 72 hours can improve recovery, and your eye may need protection to prevent damage from dryness.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Sudden one-sided facial weakness with inability to fully close the eye and mouth droop can be caused by Bell’s palsy (often after a recent viral illness), but a stroke can sometimes look similar. Because this started today and involves new facial weakness, it needs urgent evaluation to rule out stroke and to start time-sensitive treatment if it is Bell’s palsy (steroids work best when started early). Not being able to close the eye also risks corneal injury, so you may need eye protection (lubricating drops/ointment, taping the eyelid) right away.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Sudden one-sided facial weakness with inability to fully close the eye and mouth droop can be caused by Bell’s palsy (often after a recent viral illness), but it can also be caused by a stroke. Even without arm/leg weakness or speech problems, stroke can sometimes present mainly with facial droop. Because this started this morning (sudden onset) and the consequences of missing a stroke are serious, you should be evaluated urgently. If it is Bell’s palsy, early treatment (often within 72 hours) may improve recovery, and protecting the eye from drying out is important.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Sudden one-sided facial weakness with inability to fully close the eye can be caused by Bell’s palsy (often after a viral illness) or by a stroke. Bell’s palsy typically affects the whole side of the face (including the forehead/eye), while stroke more often spares the forehead and may come with arm/leg weakness, speech trouble, or other neurologic symptoms—but stroke can sometimes present mainly with facial droop. Because this started suddenly today and involves the face and eye, it needs urgent evaluation to rule out stroke and to start treatment if it’s Bell’s palsy (steroids work best when started early). Also, incomplete eye closure risks corneal injury and needs prompt protection.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Sudden one-sided facial weakness with inability to fully close the eye and a drooping mouth can be caused by Bell’s palsy (often after a viral illness), but it can also be caused by a stroke. Even without arm/leg weakness, speech problems, or headache, stroke cannot be safely ruled out from this information alone. Because this started this morning (new, sudden neurologic symptom), it needs urgent evaluation to confirm the cause and start the right treatment. If it is Bell’s palsy, early treatment may help, and protecting the eye from drying out is important. If it is a stroke, time-sensitive treatment may be needed.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 78%
